title: Complex Diabetes Management in Advanced (G5 Non-Dialysis) CKD
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: GLP-1 RA vs SGLT2i vs linagliptin at eGFR 14 — why not to combine incretin therapies, hypoglycaemia-first targets, safe insulin de-escalation, and the wider CKD-G5 agenda
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

relatedChapters.title: Type 3c Diabetes with ABPA and Chronic Pancreatitis
Statins & Fenofibrate in Diabetic Macular Edema

patient.chiefComplaint: Optimisation of a complex basal-bolus insulin regimen in advanced diabetic kidney disease.
pmh: Type 2 diabetes on basal-bolus insulin (glargine 18u; aspart 6-6-6u)
medications: Insulin aspart 6 units three times daily with meals

caseDescription: A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, CAD, hypertension and obesity (BMI 32) has advanced diabetic kidney disease — eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73m², CKD G5, not yet on dialysis. He is on a complex basal-bolus insulin regimen (glargine 18u; aspart 6-6-6u) with attendant hypoglycaemia and adherence burden. The teaching question is how to rationalise his non-insulin therapy at eGFR 14: whether linagliptin has a role, whether the resident's claim that SGLT2 inhibitors should be avoided because they are 'not evidence-based' is correct, and how to deploy a GLP-1 receptor agonist to gain cardio-renal benefit, drive weight loss, and safely de-escalate bolus insulin. The fundus is currently normal, raising the early-worsening-retinopathy caveat of rapid glycaemic correction.
keyFindings: Complex basal-bolus insulin with hypoglycaemia and injection burden
CAD + obesity (BMI 32) — strong indications for cardio-renal, weight-lowering therapy
Normal fundus — at risk of early-worsening retinopathy if HbA1c falls rapidly
finalDiagnosis: Type 2 diabetes with advanced (G5 non-dialysis) diabetic kidney disease and established CAD. Plan: set a safe, individualised glycaemic target (often HbA1c ~7-8%) and prioritise avoiding hypoglycaemia; continue/optimise basal insulin and reduce prandial insulin early; add a GLP-1 receptor agonist (dulaglutide first-line for CKD5 tolerability; semaglutide as an alternative) for weight, cardiovascular and insulin-sparing benefit; reserve linagliptin 5 mg OD as the alternative if a GLP-1 RA cannot be used — the two should NOT be combined (incretin-pathway redundancy); and withhold SGLT2i at eGFR 14 on an explicit evidence/risk-benefit basis (not because 'no evidence exists'). Look beyond glucose: kidney-protective therapy, CKD-G5 preparation, anaemia/acidosis/CKD-MBD, and CAD secondary prevention.
learningObjectives: Explain why linagliptin is the only DPP-4 inhibitor usable across all CKD stages without dose adjustment.
Correct the 'SGLT2i have no evidence in CKD' fallacy and frame the eGFR-14 decision as insufficient evidence with unfavourable risk-benefit.
Select and dose a GLP-1 receptor agonist in advanced CKD (dulaglutide/liraglutide vs semaglutide vs exenatide).
Safely de-escalate bolus insulin when adding a GLP-1 RA, and anticipate early-worsening retinopathy with rapid glycaemic correction.
Avoid combining a DPP-4 inhibitor with a GLP-1 RA, and set a safe (hypoglycaemia-avoiding) glycaemic target in CKD G5.
Manage the wider CKD-G5 agenda: kidney-protective drugs, anaemia/acidosis/CKD-MBD, dialysis/transplant preparation and CAD secondary prevention.

items.title: Why linagliptin is the only DPP-4i usable at any CKD stage
items.content: Almost all DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, vildagliptin, saxagliptin, alogliptin) are predominantly renally excreted and require stepwise dose reduction as eGFR falls — a frequent prescribing error in advanced CKD. Linagliptin is the exception: it is eliminated mainly by the hepatobiliary route, so no dose adjustment is needed at any eGFR, including dialysis. At this patient's eGFR of 14 that makes it a clean, low-risk add-on: glucose-dependent action (low intrinsic hypoglycaemia risk), weight-neutral, once daily, with a modest HbA1c reduction of ~0.5-0.8%. CARMELINA confirmed cardiovascular and kidney safety in a high-risk CKD population, and CAROLINA showed CV non-inferiority versus glimepiride with less hypoglycaemia. Crucially, linagliptin is an alternative to — not an add-on alongside — a GLP-1 receptor agonist: both act on the incretin axis, so combining them adds little and is not recommended (see the dedicated note). Use linagliptin only if a GLP-1 RA is unaffordable, not tolerated, or declined; the trade-off is that its HbA1c effect is modest and it is CV-neutral.
keyPoints: Linagliptin = hepatobiliary excretion → no renal dose adjustment, safe even on dialysis.
All other DPP-4i are renally cleared and need dose reduction in CKD.
Low hypoglycaemia risk, weight-neutral, ~0.5-0.8% HbA1c fall (CARMELINA / CAROLINA); CV-neutral.
Use as an ALTERNATIVE to a GLP-1 RA, never combined with one (incretin-pathway redundancy).

items.title: Why 'no evidence' is the wrong reason to withhold SGLT2i at eGFR 14
items.content: The resident's instinct to withhold an SGLT2 inhibitor here is defensible, but the stated reason — 'not evidence-based' — is wrong and worth correcting precisely. Two facts: (1) the glycaemic effect of SGLT2i is genuinely lost below an eGFR of ~45 because the mechanism depends on filtered glucose, so at eGFR 14 they do not lower glucose — the resident is correct on that point. (2) The cardio-renal benefit, however, is preserved at low eGFR and is squarely evidence-based in the 20-45 range: CREDENCE enrolled down to eGFR 30, DAPA-CKD to 25, and EMPA-KIDNEY to 20, each showing large reductions in kidney-disease progression. At eGFR 14 the data are extrapolated rather than absent, and the real concern is the risk side — volume depletion (on top of antihypertensives), genitourinary infection and euglycaemic DKA, with limited residual benefit if dialysis is near. The correct framing is therefore 'insufficient evidence at eGFR 14 with an unfavourable risk-benefit profile', not 'no evidence'.
keyPoints: Glycaemic benefit is truly lost <eGFR ~45 (mechanism needs filtered glucose) — resident correct here.
Cardio-renal benefit is evidence-based at eGFR 20-45 (CREDENCE 30, DAPA-CKD 25, EMPA-KIDNEY 20).
At eGFR 14: extrapolated, not absent; withhold for risk-benefit (volume depletion, GU infection, DKA), and document the reasoning.

items.title: How to choose and dose a GLP-1 RA in advanced CKD
items.content: A GLP-1 receptor agonist is the strategic add-on for this patient: it addresses obesity (3-6 kg loss), provides proven CV benefit for his CAD, has renal-protective data, can eliminate the bolus insulin burden, and carries low intrinsic hypoglycaemia risk. Agent choice matters at eGFR 14. Dulaglutide is preferred: REWIND enrolled patients down to eGFR ≥15 and showed MACE reduction plus eGFR preservation, with a tolerable GI profile. Liraglutide (LEADER, min eGFR 15) is an alternative but its nausea → dehydration → AKI risk warrants caution and data at eGFR 14 are limited. Semaglutide is not primarily renally cleared and technically needs no dose adjustment, but the landmark renal trial FLOW (2024, ~24% reduction in the kidney composite) excluded eGFR <50 — an important caveat. Exenatide is renally excreted and contraindicated below eGFR 30 — avoid. Across all GLP-1 RA the dominant practical hazard in advanced CKD is GI intolerance causing volume depletion and pre-renal AKI.
keyPoints: Dulaglutide preferred at eGFR 14-15 (REWIND included eGFR ≥15; MACE + eGFR preservation, better GI tolerance).
Semaglutide: not renally cleared, but FLOW excluded eGFR <50; liraglutide ok with caution; exenatide avoid (<30 contraindicated).
Main risk in advanced CKD = nausea/vomiting → volume depletion → AKI.

items.title: How to taper bolus insulin safely when adding a GLP-1 RA
items.content: Insulin simplification is a legitimate therapeutic goal: complex basal-bolus regimens drive hypoglycaemia, and in advanced CKD insulin clearance falls so requirements often decline. The error to avoid is adding a GLP-1 RA while leaving full bolus doses in place — that produces hypoglycaemia, not because of the GLP-1 RA but because the insulin was not reduced. The size of the first cut depends on current control: if HbA1c is near target or hypoglycaemia is already occurring, halve the prandial dose immediately (6-6-6 → 3-3-3); if HbA1c is very high without hypoglycaemia, reduce more cautiously (~20-30%) and review in 1-2 weeks. A staged plan for this patient: Week 0, start dulaglutide 0.75 mg weekly and cut bolus to 3-3-3; Week 1-2, review glucose logs and, if post-prandials are controlled, reduce further (2-2-2 or correction-only); Week 4-8, consider stopping fixed bolus entirely and reassess the basal dose, which often also needs lowering as insulin clearance falls. Continue basal insulin throughout (optimise first), and increase SMBG frequency during the transition. Complete bolus withdrawal is not guaranteed — it depends on beta-cell reserve, meal pattern and baseline HbA1c.
keyPoints: Reduce bolus pre-emptively when starting a GLP-1 RA — do not wait for hypoglycaemia.
First cut is control-dependent: ~50% (6-6-6 → 3-3-3) if near target/hypoglycaemic, ~20-30% if HbA1c very high; then taper to correction-only, reassessing basal as insulin clearance falls.
Optimise/continue basal insulin first; intensify SMBG during transition.

items.title: Why a normal fundus does not make rapid glycaemic correction safe
items.content: Counterintuitively, a normal fundus is not full reassurance. Rapid lowering of a chronically high HbA1c can precipitate early worsening of diabetic retinopathy — a transient phenomenon driven by haemodynamic and growth-factor shifts as glycaemia normalises. SUSTAIN-6 recorded an excess of retinopathy complications in the semaglutide arm, attributed largely to the magnitude and speed of HbA1c reduction in patients with pre-existing retinopathy. The practical implication when a GLP-1 RA plus linagliptin may drop HbA1c substantially: correct gradually rather than abruptly, and arrange ophthalmology review/baseline fundus documentation if a large fall (e.g. >2% absolute, or starting HbA1c >8.5%) is anticipated. The pitfall is assuming 'fundus normal = no retinal risk' and pushing glucose down quickly.
keyPoints: Rapid HbA1c fall can cause early-worsening retinopathy even when the baseline fundus is normal.
SUSTAIN-6 showed increased retinopathy events linked to rapid, large glycaemic improvement.
Correct gradually; loop in ophthalmology if a large HbA1c drop is expected.

items.title: How to apply the CKD-diabetes drug-selection framework by eGFR band
items.content: A simple eGFR-banded mental model prevents most prescribing errors. eGFR ≥45: all agents available. eGFR 30-45: SGLT2i give renal (not glycaemic) benefit; GLP-1 RA retain full benefit; most DPP-4i need dose reduction. eGFR 15-30: SGLT2i may still be considered for cardio-renal benefit; among GLP-1 RA favour dulaglutide or liraglutide; among DPP-4i only linagliptin needs no adjustment. eGFR <15 (this patient): withhold SGLT2i for insufficient data/unfavourable risk-benefit, use dulaglutide with attention to GI tolerance, use only linagliptin among DPP-4i, and expect insulin requirements to fall (reduce doses, watch hypoglycaemia). Metformin is contraindicated at this eGFR. Sulfonylureas (especially long-acting) carry high hypoglycaemia risk in CKD and are best avoided.
keyPoints: eGFR 15-30: SGLT2i for cardio-renal; GLP-1 = dulaglutide/liraglutide; DPP-4i = linagliptin only.
eGFR <15: withhold SGLT2i, dulaglutide (caution GI), linagliptin only, reduce insulin.
Avoid metformin (<30 contraindicated) and long-acting sulfonylureas in advanced CKD.

items.title: The final integrated management plan for this patient
items.content: Putting it together for a 65-year-old man, eGFR 14, BMI 32, CAD and HTN on basal-bolus insulin: (1) Set a safe, individualised glycaemic target (often HbA1c ~7-8%) and make hypoglycaemia avoidance the priority. (2) Continue and optimise basal insulin glargine 18u, and reduce prandial insulin early (≈50%, 6-6-6 → 3-3-3) once incretin therapy starts. (3) Add a GLP-1 receptor agonist as the key add-on — dulaglutide 0.75 mg weekly first-line for CKD5 tolerability (uptitrate to 1.5 mg), or semaglutide as an alternative — for CV, weight and insulin-sparing benefit. (4) Reserve linagliptin 5 mg OD only if a GLP-1 RA cannot be used, and do NOT combine the two. (5) Withhold SGLT2i at eGFR 14, documenting the reasoning and a plan to reassess (empagliflozin 10 mg would be the choice if eGFR were ≥20). (6) Correct glycaemia gradually and arrange ophthalmology review given the normal fundus. (7) Address the wider CKD-G5 agenda: continue ACEi/ARB unless intolerant, avoid metformin/sulfonylureas/pioglitazone, manage anaemia/acidosis/CKD-MBD, prepare for dialysis/transplant, and ensure CAD secondary prevention. Throughout, monitor weight, HbA1c, renal function, hydration and GI tolerance.
keyPoints: Continue basal insulin + cut prandial ~50% early; a GLP-1 RA (dulaglutide) is the add-on; linagliptin only as an alternative (never combined).
Withhold SGLT2i (document reasoning, reassess); correct glycaemia gradually + ophthalmology review.
Look beyond glucose: ACEi/ARB, avoid metformin/SU/pioglitazone, anaemia/acidosis/CKD-MBD, dialysis/transplant prep, CAD prevention.

items.question: Why can linagliptin be used at eGFR 14 without dose adjustment when sitagliptin cannot?
items.answer: Because linagliptin is eliminated predominantly by the hepatobiliary (biliary/faecal) route, its plasma levels are essentially unaffected by renal function, so no dose reduction is required at any eGFR — including dialysis. Sitagliptin and the other DPP-4 inhibitors are predominantly renally excreted, so they accumulate as eGFR falls and require stepwise dose reduction to avoid excess exposure.

items.question: How should bolus insulin be adjusted at the moment a GLP-1 RA is started in this patient?
items.answer: Reduce it pre-emptively rather than waiting for hypoglycaemia. Start dulaglutide 0.75 mg weekly and cut each mealtime aspart dose by about half immediately (6-6-6 → 3-3-3) if HbA1c is near target or hypoglycaemia is present (or only ~20-30% if HbA1c is very high without hypoglycaemia). Review glucose logs at 1-2 weeks and reduce further (→ 2-2-2 or correction-only) if post-prandial values are controlled, aim to stop fixed bolus by ~4-8 weeks, and reassess the basal dose since insulin clearance is reduced in advanced CKD. Increase SMBG frequency during the transition.

items.question: A resident says 'SGLT2 inhibitors have no evidence in CKD — avoid.' Critique this statement in three points.
items.answer: 1) Evidence clearly EXISTS for eGFR 20-45 — CREDENCE (down to 30), DAPA-CKD (down to 25) and EMPA-KIDNEY (down to 20) all showed cardio-renal benefit. 2) At eGFR 14 the evidence is limited/extrapolated, not absent. 3) The correct reason to withhold here is 'insufficient evidence at eGFR 14 with an unfavourable risk-benefit profile' (loss of glycaemic effect, volume-depletion/GU-infection/DKA risk, limited benefit if dialysis is near) — not 'no evidence'.

items.question: Why is dulaglutide a reasonable GLP-1 RA choice at eGFR 14-15 whereas exenatide is not?
items.answer: Dulaglutide was studied down to eGFR ≥15 in REWIND (MACE reduction and eGFR preservation) and is not dependent on renal clearance, with a relatively tolerable GI profile. Exenatide is renally excreted and accumulates as eGFR falls; it is contraindicated below eGFR 30 and is therefore inappropriate at eGFR 14.

items.stem: A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73m² needs an oral agent added. Why is linagliptin preferred over sitagliptin in this setting?
options: It is more potent at lowering HbA1c
It requires no renal dose adjustment because it is eliminated by the hepatobiliary route
It produces clinically meaningful weight loss
It has proven superiority for cardiovascular outcomes over sitagliptin
items.explanation: Linagliptin is excreted predominantly via the hepatobiliary route, so no dose adjustment is needed at any eGFR (including dialysis). Other DPP-4 inhibitors such as sitagliptin are renally cleared and require dose reduction. Linagliptin is weight-neutral and offers only a modest HbA1c reduction, and has not shown CV superiority over other gliptins.

items.stem: What was the minimum eGFR for enrolment in the REWIND trial of dulaglutide — the figure that supports its use in this patient?
options: 30 mL/min/1.73m²
25 mL/min/1.73m²
20 mL/min/1.73m²
15 mL/min/1.73m²
items.explanation: REWIND enrolled patients down to an eGFR of ≥15 mL/min/1.73m² and showed MACE reduction with eGFR preservation, which is why dulaglutide is a reasonable choice in advanced CKD near this patient's eGFR of 14.

items.stem: The FLOW trial (2024) of semaglutide demonstrated which of the following?
options: Safety of empagliflozin at eGFR <20
A ~24% reduction in a kidney (renal composite) outcome with semaglutide
Superiority of linagliptin over sitagliptin for renal endpoints
Elimination of hypoglycaemia risk when added to basal-bolus insulin
items.explanation: FLOW was the first dedicated renal-outcomes trial of a GLP-1 RA and showed roughly a 24% relative reduction in the renal composite with semaglutide. Note the caveat that FLOW excluded patients with eGFR <50, so it does not directly support use at eGFR 14.

items.stem: For this patient (eGFR 14, CAD, BMI 32, on basal-bolus insulin), which single add-on offers the MOST comprehensive benefit across cardiovascular risk, weight, renal protection and insulin simplification?
options: Empagliflozin
Sitagliptin
Dulaglutide
Metformin
items.explanation: Dulaglutide simultaneously addresses CV risk (REWIND MACE reduction), weight (loss in an obese patient), renal protection and allows bolus-insulin de-escalation. Empagliflozin loses glycaemic effect and has unfavourable risk-benefit at eGFR 14; sitagliptin needs dose adjustment and adds little beyond glycaemia; metformin is contraindicated at this eGFR.

items.statement: SGLT2 inhibitors provide meaningful glucose-lowering at an eGFR of 14 mL/min/1.73m².
items.explanation: False. The glucose-lowering action of SGLT2 inhibitors depends on filtered glucose and is largely lost below an eGFR of ~45, so at eGFR 14 they do not meaningfully lower glucose. Their cardio-renal benefit is a separate, eGFR-independent effect (evidence-based down to ~20) but the glycaemic effect is gone.

items.assertion: An SGLT2 inhibitor should be withheld in this patient at eGFR 14.
items.reason: There is no evidence that SGLT2 inhibitors provide any benefit in chronic kidney disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is true (withholding at eGFR 14 is reasonable on a risk-benefit basis), but the reason is false: SGLT2 inhibitors have robust cardio-renal evidence in CKD for eGFR 20-45 (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY). The decision to withhold rests on insufficient evidence and unfavourable risk-benefit specifically at eGFR 14, not on an absence of evidence in CKD. Hence A is true, R is false (option 2).

items.title: Why avoiding hypoglycaemia outranks tight control at eGFR 14
items.content: In CKD G5 the safety priority flips: hypoglycaemia, not hyperglycaemia, is the immediate threat. Renal insulin clearance falls, gluconeogenesis by the failing kidney declines, appetite and intake fluctuate, and many agents accumulate — so the same regimen that was safe earlier now causes lows. For a 65-year-old with CAD (where hypoglycaemia can precipitate arrhythmia and ischaemia) and advanced CKD, the sensible target is safe rather than tight control — commonly HbA1c around 7-8%, individualised, with explicit avoidance of hypoglycaemia. Chasing a near-normal HbA1c here is a classic resident error that trades a small microvascular gain for a real risk of harm.
keyPoints: CKD5 lowers insulin clearance and renal gluconeogenesis → higher hypoglycaemia risk on an unchanged regimen.
Target safe control (often HbA1c ~7-8%, individualised), not tight control.
Hypoglycaemia is especially dangerous with coexisting CAD — avoid it deliberately.

items.title: Why HbA1c can mislead in CKD5 — and what to monitor instead
items.content: HbA1c is often unreliable in advanced CKD: anaemia, shortened red-cell survival, iron deficiency, recent transfusion and erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) use all distort it, and it can read falsely low or high. Relying on HbA1c alone risks both under- and over-treatment. The resident should triangulate: structured SMBG (fasting, pre-meal, 2-hour post-meal, bedtime) or — preferably — continuous glucose monitoring, alongside the haemoglobin/iron/ESA context. A full baseline also includes hypoglycaemia frequency, UACR/albuminuria, potassium, bicarbonate, calcium/phosphate/PTH, and current BP with ACEi/ARB/diuretic use, because these shape every subsequent drug decision.
keyPoints: Anaemia, altered RBC survival, transfusion and ESA use make HbA1c unreliable in CKD5.
Use SMBG (fasting/pre-meal/2-h post-meal/bedtime) or CGM, interpreted with the haematology context.
Baseline work-up: hypoglycaemia history, UACR, K, HCO3, Ca/PO4/PTH, BP and RAAS/diuretic use.

items.title: Why you must not combine linagliptin with a GLP-1 RA
items.content: DPP-4 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists act on the same incretin pathway: DPP-4 inhibitors work by preventing the breakdown of endogenous GLP-1, raising it to near-physiological levels, whereas a GLP-1 RA delivers pharmacological receptor agonism directly. Adding a DPP-4 inhibitor on top of a GLP-1 RA therefore adds essentially no further glycaemic benefit while adding cost and pill burden, and is not recommended by guidelines. The clean decision rule for this patient: choose one incretin-based therapy. If a GLP-1 RA is being used (the preferred route here for its weight, CV and insulin-sparing benefits), do not add linagliptin; reserve linagliptin for when a GLP-1 RA cannot be used at all.
keyPoints: DPP-4i raise endogenous GLP-1; GLP-1 RA give direct pharmacological agonism — overlapping mechanism.
Combining them adds little glycaemic benefit, only cost/burden — not recommended.
Pick one incretin therapy: GLP-1 RA preferred here; linagliptin only if a GLP-1 RA is not an option.

items.title: How to screen a patient before starting a GLP-1 RA
items.content: Before the first dose, run a quick safety screen. Contraindications/cautions: personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 (avoid), prior pancreatitis, severe gastroparesis, active gallbladder disease, and frailty/malnutrition or recurrent vomiting/poor intake. The CKD5-specific hazard is that GI adverse effects (nausea, vomiting, reduced intake) cause dehydration, pre-renal AKI and — if insulin is not reduced — hypoglycaemia. Hence the operating rules: start low, go slow, reduce insulin early, counsel hard on hydration and GI symptoms, and monitor glucose and renal function closely. The normal fundus is reassuring, but avoid a sudden large fall in glucose and continue annual eye review (early-worsening retinopathy risk).
keyPoints: Screen for MTC/MEN2 (avoid), pancreatitis, severe gastroparesis, gallbladder disease, frailty/poor intake.
CKD5 risk chain: GI upset → dehydration → AKI, and hypoglycaemia if insulin not cut.
Start low, go slow, reduce insulin early, counsel on hydration; avoid abrupt large HbA1c falls (retinopathy).

items.title: How to handle the other glucose-lowering and kidney drugs in CKD5
items.content: Beyond the incretin/SGLT2i question, residents must know the fate of the remaining classes at eGFR 14. Metformin is contraindicated (lactic-acidosis risk) — do not use. Sulfonylureas (especially long-acting) carry a high hypoglycaemia risk in CKD5 and are best avoided. Pioglitazone causes fluid retention, weight gain and heart-failure risk — avoid/caution, particularly with CAD. Finerenone is a useful kidney-protective agent in diabetic CKD but is generally not initiated at eGFR 14 — initiation usually requires eGFR ≥25 with a normal potassium. ACEi/ARB should usually be continued for kidney and CV protection: do not stop them merely because eGFR is low, unless there is hyperkalaemia, AKI, symptomatic hypotension or intolerance.
keyPoints: Metformin contraindicated; avoid sulfonylureas (hypoglycaemia) and pioglitazone (fluid/HF) in CKD5.
Finerenone usually needs eGFR ≥25 + normal K — not a starting drug at eGFR 14.
Continue ACEi/ARB unless hyperkalaemia, AKI, hypotension or intolerance — not just because eGFR is low.

items.title: Why eGFR 14 means managing far more than glucose
items.content: At eGFR 14 the patient is approaching kidney replacement therapy, and a glucose-only focus is a serious omission. The parallel agenda: timely nephrology follow-up; dialysis modality education (haemodialysis vs peritoneal dialysis); arteriovenous fistula planning ahead of need if haemodialysis is likely; transplant evaluation if a candidate; hepatitis B vaccination before dialysis; and strict avoidance of nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, unnecessary contrast). Framing the case as 'which diabetes drug' alone misses that the dominant determinant of this man's outcome over the next 1-2 years is how well his transition to kidney replacement therapy and his cardiovascular risk are managed.
keyPoints: Refer/continue nephrology; educate on dialysis modality; plan AV fistula early if HD likely.
Assess for transplant; give hepatitis B vaccination before dialysis.
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, unnecessary contrast) — protect residual function.

items.title: How to manage the CKD complications and CAD secondary prevention
items.content: Two further workstreams run alongside glycaemia. CKD complications: check haemoglobin and iron studies and treat deficiency/assess ESA need for anaemia; measure bicarbonate and treat metabolic acidosis if low; monitor potassium (especially on RAAS blockade); and manage CKD-mineral and bone disorder (calcium, phosphate, PTH, vitamin D) and overall nutrition/albumin. CAD secondary prevention: a high-intensity statin (or statin plus ezetimibe) if tolerated, antiplatelet therapy if indicated, blood-pressure control, smoking cessation, and beta-blocker/antianginal therapy as indicated. In a CKD5 patient with established CAD, these interventions often matter as much as the choice of glucose-lowering agent.
keyPoints: CKD-MBD and complications: anaemia/iron/ESA, acidosis (HCO3), potassium, Ca/PO4/PTH/vitD, nutrition.
CAD prevention: high-intensity statin ± ezetimibe, antiplatelet if indicated, BP control, beta-blocker/antianginal.
These often influence prognosis as much as the diabetes drug choice.

items.title: How to use CGM (or structured SMBG) to guide insulin reduction in CKD5
items.content: Because this man is on insulin and in CKD5 — the highest-risk combination for hypoglycaemia — continuous glucose monitoring is strongly useful where available: it detects silent and nocturnal hypoglycaemia that fingersticks miss and gives the data to safely down-titrate insulin as the GLP-1 RA takes effect. Where CGM is not available, use a structured fingerstick schedule (fasting, pre-meal, 2-hour post-meal, bedtime) and intensify it during the transition. The monitoring is not an add-on luxury here; it is what makes aggressive insulin de-escalation safe.
keyPoints: CGM is high-value in CKD5-on-insulin: catches silent/nocturnal lows and guides safe insulin reduction.
If no CGM: structured SMBG (fasting, pre-meal, 2-h post-meal, bedtime), intensified during transition.
Good monitoring is what makes bolus de-escalation safe — not optional.

items.question: Why is HbA1c an unreliable glycaemic measure in this CKD5 patient?
items.answer: Advanced CKD distorts the relationship between HbA1c and average glucose: anaemia, shortened red-cell survival, iron deficiency, recent transfusion and erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) use all alter glycation and red-cell turnover, so HbA1c can read falsely low (or sometimes high). Management should therefore rest on SMBG or CGM data interpreted alongside the haematological context, not on HbA1c alone.

items.question: How should ACEi/ARB and finerenone be handled in this patient at eGFR 14?
items.answer: Continue the ACEi/ARB for kidney and cardiovascular protection — do not stop it merely because eGFR is low; stop only for hyperkalaemia, AKI, symptomatic hypotension or intolerance, and check potassium/renal function. Do not initiate finerenone at eGFR 14: it is generally started only at eGFR ≥25 with a normal potassium, so it is outside its evidence/label window here.

items.question: What glycaemic target is appropriate for this 65-year-old with CAD and CKD5, and why?
items.answer: A safe, individualised target — commonly HbA1c around 7-8% — rather than tight control, with explicit avoidance of hypoglycaemia. The rationale: in CKD5 insulin clearance and renal gluconeogenesis fall and intake fluctuates, raising hypoglycaemia risk, and hypoglycaemia is especially hazardous with coexisting CAD. HbA1c is also unreliable in CKD, so the target must be interpreted alongside SMBG/CGM.

items.stem: A resident proposes starting dulaglutide AND adding linagliptin for extra glycaemic effect in this patient. Why is this combination not recommended?
options: Linagliptin requires renal dose adjustment that dulaglutide does not
DPP-4 inhibitors and GLP-1 RAs act on the same incretin pathway, so combining them adds little benefit while increasing cost
The combination markedly increases the risk of lactic acidosis
DPP-4 inhibitors block the absorption of injectable GLP-1 RAs
items.explanation: DPP-4 inhibitors raise endogenous GLP-1 by blocking its degradation, while GLP-1 RAs provide direct pharmacological receptor agonism — the same pathway. Adding a DPP-4 inhibitor on top of a GLP-1 RA yields negligible extra glycaemic benefit and only adds cost and burden, so it is not recommended. Choose one incretin-based therapy.

items.stem: Which statement about non-incretin glucose-lowering and kidney-protective drugs is correct at this patient's eGFR of 14?
options: Metformin is safe to continue at eGFR 14 if the dose is halved
Finerenone should be initiated now for kidney protection
Metformin is contraindicated, and finerenone is generally not initiated below eGFR 25
Long-acting sulfonylureas are the preferred oral agents in CKD5
items.explanation: Metformin is contraindicated at eGFR 14 (lactic-acidosis risk). Finerenone is a useful agent in diabetic CKD but is generally initiated only at eGFR ≥25 with normal potassium, so not here. Long-acting sulfonylureas carry a high hypoglycaemia risk in CKD5 and should be avoided.

items.stem: Beyond glucose-lowering, which of the following is the MOST appropriate priority in this eGFR-14 patient?
options: Start an NSAID for his joint pains to improve mobility
Stop the ACEi/ARB because eGFR is low
Nephrology referral with dialysis-modality education and early AV-fistula planning
Initiate empagliflozin for guaranteed renal protection
items.explanation: At eGFR 14 the dominant agenda is preparation for kidney replacement therapy: nephrology follow-up, dialysis-modality education, early AV-fistula planning, transplant assessment and hepatitis B vaccination. NSAIDs are nephrotoxic and should be avoided; the ACEi/ARB should usually be continued (not stopped just because eGFR is low); and empagliflozin should not be newly started at eGFR 14.

items.statement: An ACE inhibitor or ARB should be stopped simply because this patient's eGFR has fallen to 14.
items.explanation: False. A low eGFR alone is not a reason to stop RAAS blockade; ACEi/ARB are continued for kidney and cardiovascular protection. They are stopped only for specific problems — hyperkalaemia, acute kidney injury, symptomatic hypotension or intolerance — with monitoring of potassium and renal function.

items.assertion: Linagliptin should not be added for a patient who is being started on a GLP-1 receptor agonist.
items.reason: DPP-4 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists both act on the incretin system, so combining them provides little additional glycaemic benefit.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the mechanism in the reason explains the recommendation in the assertion: because DPP-4 inhibitors (which raise endogenous GLP-1) and GLP-1 RAs (direct receptor agonists) act on the same incretin pathway, adding one to the other yields negligible extra benefit, so the combination is avoided. Hence option 0.

items.stem: Why can linagliptin be used at an eGFR of 14 mL/min/1.73 m² without dose adjustment, unlike sitagliptin?
options: A. Linagliptin is renally excreted but has a longer half-life, requiring no dose adjustment even at severe renal impairment
B. Sitagliptin is primarily metabolized by the liver, whereas linagliptin is renally excreted
C. Linagliptin is eliminated predominantly via the hepatobiliary route, making its plasma concentrations independent of renal function
D. Both drugs are excreted renally, but linagliptin has higher protein binding that prevents accumulation
items.explanation: Linagliptin's primary elimination pathway is hepatic (biliary/faecal), so renal function does not affect its plasma levels. Sitagliptin and other DPP-4 inhibitors are predominantly renally excreted, leading to accumulation in renal impairment and requiring dose reductions.

items.stem: How should bolus insulin be adjusted when initiating a GLP-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) in a patient with type 2 diabetes?
options: A) Wait for hypoglycaemia before reducing bolus insulin doses
B) Reduce mealtime aspart doses by 50% immediately if HbA1c is near target, regardless of hypoglycaemia history
C) Maintain current bolus insulin doses and adjust only if post-prandial glucose exceeds 180 mg/dL
D) Discontinue bolus insulin entirely and rely on GLP-1 RA for glucose control
items.explanation: GLP-1 RAs enhance insulin secretion and suppress glucagon, increasing hypoglycaemia risk when combined with insulin. Proactive bolus insulin reduction (e.g., 50% decrease in mealtime doses if HbA1c is near target) is recommended to prevent hypoglycaemia, with further adjustments based on glucose monitoring.

items.stem: Which statement best critiques the claim that 'SGLT2 inhibitors have no evidence in CKD — avoid'?
options: A) Evidence exists for eGFR 20-45 (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY), limited evidence at eGFR 14, and the correct reason to withhold is 'insufficient evidence with unfavourable risk-benefit profile'
B) Evidence is absent for eGFR <30, and the risk of DKA precludes use in CKD
C) SGLT2 inhibitors are contraindicated in CKD due to increased risk of acute kidney injury
D) The only evidence for SGLT2 inhibitors in CKD is in eGFR >60, and they should be avoided in lower eGFR ranges
items.explanation: CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, and EMPA-KIDNEY demonstrate cardio-renal benefits in eGFR 20-45. Evidence at eGFR 14 is limited, not absent, and withholding should be based on insufficient evidence and risk-benefit profile, not absence of evidence.

items.stem: Why is dulaglutide a reasonable GLP-1 RA choice at eGFR 14-15 whereas exenatide is not?
options: A) Dulaglutide was studied down to eGFR ≥15 in REWIND and is not dependent on renal clearance, whereas exenatide is renally excreted and accumulates at low eGFR
B) Dulaglutide has greater MACE reduction efficacy in renal impairment compared to exenatide
C) Exenatide is preferred in patients with eGFR <30 due to lower GI side effects
D) Dulaglutide is contraindicated below eGFR 30, unlike exenatide
items.explanation: Dulaglutide's safety and efficacy in eGFR ≥15 (REWIND trial) and lack of renal dependence make it suitable at eGFR 14-15. Exenatide's renal excretion leads to accumulation and contraindication below eGFR 30.

items.stem: Why is HbA1c an unreliable glycaemic measure in a patient with CKD stage 5?
options: A) Advanced CKD distorts the relationship between HbA1c and average glucose due to anaemia, shortened red-cell survival, iron deficiency, recent transfusion, and ESA use
B) Diabetes duration >10 years interferes with HbA1c glycation kinetics
C) Recurrent hypoglycaemia in type 1 diabetes causes HbA1c to underestimate true glucose levels
D) Vitamin B12 deficiency alters HbA1c by interfering with glycosylation pathways
items.explanation: In CKD stage 5, factors like anaemia, ESA use, and altered red-cell turnover disrupt the correlation between HbA1c and average glucose, leading to potential under- or overestimation of glycaemic control.

items.stem: What is the appropriate management of ACEi/ARB and finerenone in a patient with eGFR 14?
options: A) Discontinue ACEi/ARB and initiate finerenone
B) Continue ACEi/ARB unless contraindicated; avoid finerenone due to eGFR <25
C) Discontinue both ACEi/ARB and finerenone
D) Initiate finerenone and continue ACEi/ARB
items.explanation: ACEi/ARB should be continued for renal/cardiovascular protection unless contraindicated (e.g., hyperkalemia, AKI). Finerenone is contraindicated at eGFR <25 due to limited evidence and label restrictions.

items.stem: What is the appropriate glycaemic target for a 65-year-old patient with coronary artery disease (CAD) and stage 5 chronic kidney disease (CKD5)?
options: A) HbA1c <6.5% with intensive glucose monitoring
B) HbA1c 7-8% with avoidance of hypoglycaemia and use of SMBG/CGM
C) HbA1c >8.5% to prevent hypoglycaemia
D) HbA1c 6-7% with frequent insulin dose adjustments
items.explanation: In CKD5, insulin clearance and renal gluconeogenesis are impaired, increasing hypoglycaemia risk, which is particularly hazardous in CAD. HbA1c is unreliable in CKD, necessitating SMBG/CGM alongside a less stringent target (7-8%) to balance glycaemic control and safety.
